Awesome Game Solving

The canonical reference for solved games: every known result, the method that produced it, and the code to verify it.

36 solved games · 17 strongly solved · 11 genres · citation-gated · source & contribute on GitHub →

New · original result

We strongly solved six men's morris — a draw over all 42,372,745 reachable positions — and built an interactive perfect-play explorer for the family.

Open the explorer →

What "solved" means

  • Ultra-weak — the value of the starting position is known (who wins, or draw), but not how.
  • Weak — the value and a strategy to achieve it from the start are known.
  • Strong — the value is known for every reachable position, not just the start (a full tablebase).
GameResultStrength MethodYearGenreSource
Three men's morris3×3 grid of 9 points (3 horizontal + 3 vertical + 2 diagonal lines), 3 pieces per side First player wins strong Retrograde analysis — Morris / mill family folklore
Tic-tac-toe3×3 (m,n,k = 3,3,3) Draw strong Brute-force enumeration — Alignment & m,n,k games folklore
Maharajah and the SepoysStandard 8x8 chessboard. Black ("sepoys") has the full orthodox 16-piece army in the normal starting position; White has a single piece, the maharajah, starting on e1, that moves on its turn as either a queen or a knight (the "amazon" compound). The maharajah is royal (must escape check); Black's pawns cannot promote in the usual rendering. No castling for the lone maharajah. Second player wins weak Knowledge-based 1892 Hunt & unequal forces Falkener 1892
NimSeveral heaps (piles) of counters; players alternately remove one or more counters from a single heap. Classic three-heap demonstration uses heaps of 3, 5, 7. First player wins strong Mathematical proof 1901 Combinatorial game theory Bouton 1901
Wythoff's gameTwo heaps of counters (unbounded, non-negative integer sizes) First player wins strong Mathematical proof 1907 Combinatorial game theory Wythoff 1907
Hexapawn3x3 (standard); generalizes to n x m rectangular boards (e.g. 4x4 'octopawn') Second player wins strong Brute-force enumeration 1962 Chess & chess variants Gardner 1962
SimComplete graph K6 (6 vertices, 15 edges); each player colors edges in their own color Second player wins strong Brute-force enumeration 1974 Combinatorial game theory Mead et al. 1974
Qubic4×4×4 cube (64 cells, 76 winning lines) First player wins weak Knowledge-based 1980 Alignment & m,n,k games Patashnik 1980
Mū tōrereEight-pointed star: 8 outer points (kewai) arranged in a ring, each joined to its two neighbours and to a central point (pūtahi); 9 cells total. Four pieces per player, placed on the eight outer points (the centre starts empty). Draw strong Brute-force enumeration 1987 Capture & board control Ascher 1987
Gomoku (free-style)15×15 First player wins weak Threat-space search 1993 Alignment & m,n,k games Allis et al. 1993
Nine Men's Morris24-point morris board (three concentric squares joined by midpoint connectors), 9 stones per player Draw strong Retrograde analysis 1993 Morris / mill family Gasser 1993
Connect Four7x6 (7 columns wide, 6 rows high), gravity drop First player wins strong Brute-force enumeration 1995 Alignment & m,n,k games Tromp 1995
Pentominoes (two-player)8×8 board, 12 distinct (free) pentomino pieces First player wins weak Brute-force enumeration 1996 Combinatorial game theory Orman 1996
Teeko5×5 board (25 cells); each player has 4 markers Draw strong Retrograde analysis 1998 Alignment & m,n,k games Steele 1998
KalahTwo rows of 6 holes plus two stores (kalahah); standard commercial game starts with 4 seeds per hole — Kalah(6,4). Notation Kalah(m,n) = m holes per side, n initial seeds per hole. First player wins weak Alpha-beta + DB 2000 Mancala Irving et al. 2000
Renju (連珠)15x15 grid (stones on intersections); Black plays first and must open on the center point h8 First player wins weak Threat-space search 2001 Alignment & m,n,k games Wágner et al. 2001
Awari (Oware)2×6 pits, 4 seeds per pit (48 seeds total) Draw strong Retrograde analysis 2002 Mancala Romein et al. 2002
Go (5×5)5x5 square Go board (25 intersections) First player wins weak Alpha-beta + DB 2002 Go van der Werf 2002
Hex (7×7)7×7 rhombus of hexagonal cells (49 cells); each player owns two opposite sides, first player connects their pair; no-swap (no pie rule) ruleset First player wins weak Strategy-stealing 2003 Connection games Yang et al. 2003
Lasker MorrisNine Men's Morris board (24 points / intersections, no diagonals); 10 men per player (both Stahlhacke's headline 136-billion-position database and Gévay & Danner solve the ten-men variant, per Lasker's "nine or better ten") Draw strong Retrograde analysis 2003 Morris / mill family Stahlhacke 2003
Checkers (English draughts)8x8 board, pieces on dark squares only (32 playable squares) Draw weak Retrograde analysis 2007 Capture & board control Schaeffer et al. 2007
Fanorona5x9 (Fanoron-Tsivy), 45 intersections of lines; 22 pieces per side at start Draw weak Proof-number search 2007 Capture & board control Schadd et al. 2007
Tigers and Goats5x5 grid of 25 points connected by horizontal, vertical and diagonal lines (Bagh-Chal board). Asymmetric: 4 tigers (start on the 4 corner points) face up to 20 goats. Goat moves first. Draw weak Retrograde analysis 2007 Hunt & unequal forces Jin et al. 2007
Dōbutsu shōgi3×4 (3 files × 4 ranks) Second player wins strong Retrograde analysis 2009 Shogi variants Tanaka 2009
Gardner minichess5x5 (smallest board holding all standard chess piece types on the back rank) Draw weak Knowledge-based 2013 Chess & chess variants Mhalla et al. 2013
Hex (9×9, all openings)9×9 rhombus of hexagonal cells (81 cells); two opposite sides per player, first player connects their pair; no-swap (no pie rule) ruleset First player wins weak Depth-first PNS 2013 Connection games Pawlewicz et al. 2013
Dots and BoxesRectangular grids, sized in boxes. Headline result is the 5x5 board (5x5 = 25 boxes, played on a 6x6 array of dots). Solved instances range from tiny boards up through 4x4 (Wilson), 4x5 (Barker & Korf) and 5x5 (Fraser). First player wins weak Alpha-beta + DB 2014 Combinatorial game theory Fraser 2014
Pentago6×6 with four rotatable 3×3 quadrants First player wins strong Retrograde analysis 2014 Alignment & m,n,k games Irving 2014
Amazons (small boards)5x6 rectangular grid (headline); also solved: 4x4, 5x5, 4x5, 5x4, 4x6, 4x7, 6x4 First player wins weak Alpha-beta + DB 2015 Combinatorial game theory Song et al. 2015
Heads-up Limit Texas Hold'em (HULHE)Two-player limit Texas hold'em; 52-card deck, four betting rounds (preflop/flop/turn/river), fixed bet sizes, capped raises per round, alternating dealer/button First player wins (ε-Nash) weak (approx.) Monte-Carlo CFR 2015 Imperfect information Bowling et al. 2015
MorabarabaNine Men's Morris board with the four diagonal lines added (24 points / intersections, all corners of the three nested squares connected by diagonals); 12 men per player First player wins strong Retrograde analysis 2015 Morris / mill family Gévay 2015
DomineeringSquare and rectangular grids; headline result is 11x11. Solved instances range from 1x1 up to 11x11 squares plus many m x n rectangles. First player wins weak Alpha-beta + DB 2016 Combinatorial game theory Uiterwijk 2016
Losing chessStandard 8x8 chessboard, orthodox starting position; captures compulsory, non-royal king, no castling, promotion to king allowed First player wins weak Proof-number search 2016 Chess & chess variants Watkins 2016
Othello8x8 (64 squares; standard cross start with 4 discs placed) Draw weak Alpha-beta + DB 2023 Capture & board control Takizawa 2023
Quarto!4×4 board, 16 distinct pieces (each a unique combination of 4 binary attributes; standard line-win ruleset — rows, columns, two diagonals) Draw strong Alpha-beta + DB 2023 Alignment & m,n,k games Goossens 2023
Six men's morrisTwo concentric squares (16 points) joined by lines at the four side-midpoints; no diagonals. Equivalent to the nine men's morris board with its outer ring removed. 6 men per player. Draw strong Retrograde analysis 2026 Morris / mill family Liou 2026
No games match.
Generated from structured data — the dataset is the product. The README table and this page render from data/games/*.yaml. Unsolved games live on the frontier, never as catalog entries. Released under CC0.
